topics: How artists show people differently across history
Roles people play and what they wear
subject: Arts

sections.heading: Your Quest Begins — What Makes Every Face Unique?
sections.body: Think about your cricket team — every player has the same parts on their face, eyes, nose, mouth, ears, but each one looks totally different! That is the magic of a face. When you draw your friend's face, you become a wizard of observation. You notice if their eyes are big or small, if their forehead is wide, if they wear spectacles, or how they comb their hair. These tiny details are your spell to capture who they really are.
keyPoints: Every face has the same parts but each person looks unique because of different shapes, sizes and features
Observing details carefully — like the space between eyes or the shape of the chin — is the key power of a portrait artist
Your portrait does not have to be perfect, it just needs your own flavour and honesty

sections.heading: The Portrait Spell — Drawing Your Classmate Step by Step
sections.body: Imagine you are a wizard casting a spell to bring your friend's face to life on paper. First, you draw the shape of their face — is it round like a laddoo, oval like a mango, or egg-shaped? Then you carefully add the eyes, eyebrows, nose, lips, chin and ears, paying attention to how far apart or close together things are. After the drawing is ready, you choose colours and finally cut it out. When the whole class puts all their portraits together on one big sheet, you get a magical group portrait — like a team photo, but made by you!
keyPoints: Start with the face shape, then add features one by one — observe distances and sizes carefully
Colour your portrait thoughtfully to make it feel real and personal
Combining everyone's portraits creates a group artwork that celebrates every unique face in the class

sections.heading: History's Realm — How Artists Showed the Buddha
sections.body: Long ago, artists from different parts of India and different time periods drew and sculpted the Buddha in their own special style, just like how your favourite cartoon looks different on different channels. The Gandhara style from around 100 BCE had a Greek influence and looked very realistic. The Mathura style was bold and Indian. The Gupta style from Sarnath looked peaceful and smooth. The Thangka style from Buddhist traditions looked flat and colourful like a decorated rangoli. Same person, so many different artistic styles — that is the power of art across history!
keyPoints: Artists from different time periods and places showed the Buddha in very different styles — Gandhara, Mathura, Sarnath and Thangka
A portrait can change in look depending on the culture, time period and the artist's own style
Comparing old artworks helps us understand how people saw the world in different eras

sections.heading: Dream Big — Draw the Role You Want to Play
sections.body: Every hero in a quest has a role — some protect, some heal, some build. What role do you want to play when you grow up? Maybe you want to be a doctor keeping people healthy, or an electrician fixing lights in homes, or a cricketer bringing joy to millions. In this activity, you draw yourself in three to five future roles. There is no right or wrong answer — you can even imagine being a space explorer! Drawing your dreams is a brave and exciting spell.
keyPoints: Think about how you want to help people in your life and draw yourself doing those things
You can imagine more than one role — your future has many possibilities
Looking at your classmates' drawings helps you see the many different ways people dream of contributing to the world

sections.heading: Clothes and Identity — Does a Uniform Make the Hero?
sections.body: When you see someone in a white coat, you think doctor. When you see someone in a turban and dhoti working in a field, you think farmer. But wait — if a farmer puts on jeans and a T-shirt, are they still a farmer? Of course yes! Clothes give us clues about someone's role, but they do not define who a person truly is. Farmers can be men or women, young or old, wearing many different things. This activity teaches you to look beyond clothes and see people with fresh, open eyes — that is true mastery of observation.
keyPoints: Clothes often give hints about a person's job or role, but they do not tell the whole story
People of all genders can do any job — a farmer, a musician, a painter can look very different from what we usually imagine
Drawing the same person in different roles or clothes helps break fixed ideas about how people should look

summary: In this chapter, you went on a wonderful quest to understand how people are portrayed in art. You discovered that every face is unique even though we all have the same features, and you used your wizard-like observation skills to draw portraits of your classmates and create a big group artwork together. You then travelled through the realm of history to see how artists across different time periods showed the Buddha in totally different styles, from the realistic Gandhara style to the smooth and peaceful Sarnath style. You also dreamed about your own future roles and drew yourself as the hero you want to become. Finally, you explored how clothes give hints about people's roles but do not define them, and you learned to look at every person with open, curious and respectful eyes — that is the true mastery of an artist.
